text: The surgeon performs a partial vaginectomy to excise rectovaginal endometriosis, focusing on the removal of affected tissue from the vaginal and rectal regions.
This video will demonstrate the excision of rectovaginal endometriosis after completion of a total laparoscopic hysterectomy.
We will also describe laparoscopic techniques to perform a partial vaginectomy when the uterus is left in situ.
Lastly, we will incorporate surgical techniques and tips in our discussion.
Rectovaginal endometriosis is a form of deep infiltrating endometriosis. When it involves the rectovaginal wall, patients can present with deep dyspareunia and dyskiesia.
It commonly affects the posterior fornix and nodularity is often palpable in the posterior cul-de-sac.
The surgeon begins a partial vaginectomy to excise a three-centimeter rectovaginal nodule following the completion of a total laparoscopic hysterectomy.
The surgeon develops the rectovaginal space to mobilize the rectum below the lowest margin of the vaginal nodule, preparing for the colpotomy incision.
A xeropolysorb suture is used to tag the most distal border of the rectovaginal nodule, aiding in its localization for the upcoming laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon provides traction on the tagged nodule using blunt graspers, implementing the squeeze method to delineate the margins of the rectovaginal nodule.
The surgeon identifies that the nodule is confined to the posterior vaginal wall, visually confirming its location during the procedure.
The surgeon creates an inverted V-shaped incision on the posterior vaginal wall using monopolar shears, preparing to excise the rectovaginal nodule.
The surgeon uses an anzeal bipolar device to transect the nodule, which is located on the posterior vaginal wall.
The surgeon carefully hugs the nodule to minimize the amount of tissue transected from the posterior vaginal wall during the procedure.
The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction techniques to excise the nodule completely from the posterior vaginal wall, using blunt graspers to delineate its margins.
The surgeon begins the vaginal cuff closure by anchoring a unidirectional barb suture at the apex of the inverted V-shaped incision on the posterior vaginal wall.
The surgeon re-approximates the posterior vaginal wall along its longitudinal aspect using a running suture technique.
The surgeon adjusts the posterior vaginal wall to provide additional length, ensuring that the excision of the nodule does not result in shortening of the vagina.
The surgeon completes the closure of the vaginal cuff using a traditional transverse suturing technique, ensuring the edges are securely approximated.
The surgeon places a modified Richardson stitch using two O PDS sutures at the left vaginal cuff angle, incorporating the left utero-sacral ligament into the closure.
The surgeon places a similar stitch at the right vaginal cuff angle using a 2-0 V-Lock suture, running it continuously from right to left to incorporate the entire cut edge of the vaginal mucosa.
The surgeon begins a partial vaginectomy to excise a five-centimeter rectovaginal nodule, with the uterus left in situ to preserve the patient's future fertility.
The surgical team proceeds without placing a uterine manipulator, allowing for direct intraoperative palpation of the rectovaginal nodule.
The rigidity of the manipulator would also hinder the reconstruction of the posterior vaginal fornix.
The surgeon tags the endometriotic nodule located in the left cul-de-sac with a single interrupted suture at its most distal border, marking the area for excision.
The surgeon suspends the uterus to the anterior abdominal wall to optimize exposure of the cul-de-sac, enhancing visibility for the excision of the rectovaginal nodule.
A temporary 2-0 Prolene suture and sterile IV tubing are placed between the suture and the uterine fundus, likely to aid in surgical exposure or manipulation.
The surgeon anchors the suture to the anterior abdominal wall, facilitating the antiversion of the uterus for improved exposure during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a temporary left oophorepexy using a 2-0 proline suture on a Keith needle, securing the ovary to facilitate access to the left parametrium.
The surgeon anchors the suture to the anterior abdominal wall in the left lower quadrant, suspending the uterus to optimize exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs ureterolysis in the left cul-de-sac, addressing extensive endometriosis involving the left ovarian fossa. The nodule's location necessitates careful dissection around the ureter.
The surgeon identifies the ureter along its anatomical course, encountering severe retroperitoneal fibrosis that is visually apparent.
The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to identify the uterine artery at its origin, while developing the pararectal space.
Given the large size of the endometriotic nodule and to minimize bleeding during completion of the ureterolysis,
The surgeon skeletonizes the left uterine artery and its vaginal branches, temporarily ligating them with 5mm endoclips, visibly isolating these vessels from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon safely mobilizes the left ureter further laterally, navigating through the retroperitoneal space with visible care to avoid surrounding fibrotic tissue.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision medially to develop the left pararectal fossa, further expanding the pararectal space adjacent to the colorectal surgeon's area.
An incision is made medial to the right uterus acral ligament to develop the right parorectal fossa, mirroring the approach used on the left side.
The surgeon develops the rectovaginal space and mobilizes the rectum away from the endometriotic nodule and posterior vagina using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques.

The surgeon places a Bryski-Navratol retractor in the posterior vagina to expose the area obstructed by the endometriotic nodule. Monopolar shears are used to initiate a colpotomy in the posterior fornix.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the endometriotic nodule from the posterior cervix, separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the previously tagged suture within the surgical field, ensuring its position is noted for subsequent steps in the procedure.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to nucleate the tagged endometriotic nodule from its attachment, employing traction and countertraction techniques.

The surgeon reconstructs the large colpotomy incision, reattaching it to the patient's posterior cervix. A two-layer closure is performed in a running fashion using a unidirectional barbed suture to recreate the posterior fornix.

The surgeon completes a two-layer closure of the vaginal mucosa, with the first layer incorporating the cut edge of the vaginal mucosa and the second layer serving as an imprecating layer using a second stitch of the same suture.
The surgeon removes the previously placed endoclips, revealing excellent hemostasis in the surgical area.
Partial vaginectomy for excision of rectovaginal endometriosis can be performed conservatively with the uterus left in situ or after completion of the hysterectomy to allow for excellent exposure.
The surgeon tags the nodule vaginally, which helps delineate its borders, facilitating precise dissection of the tissue.
The surgeon temporarily suspends the uterus and ovaries to the abdominal wall, enhancing exposure for the procedure in the absence of a uterine manipulator.
The surgeon employs advanced laparoscopic suturing techniques to reconstruct the vaginal tissue, carefully aligning and securing the layers.
